name: Wheel of Heaven Translation Glossary
description: Lexical decisions made by the Wheel of Heaven Translation Program. Distinct from content/i18n/glossary.json, which records site-UI terminology. Each entry records a source-language term, the standard PD-translation rendering, the WoH choice, the rationale, and the verses where the choice applies. Pinned to per-chapter translations via `glossaryVersion` in chapter metadata.
scopeNote: v1.0.0 covered Genesis 1; v2.5.0 added Job 1-2. v2.6.0 adds Job 38 (10 entries) — the opening of YHWH's first speech from the whirlwind. Keystone entries: bnei-elohim-yarinu (the third Hebrew Bible attestation of the bnei elohim, at the moment of cosmic founding) and kimah-kesil-mazzarot-ayish (the Hebrew Bible's canonical four-named astronomical-constellation catalogue — Pleiades, Orion, the Zodiac, the Great Bear). The chapter is the densest WoH-cosmology-relevant section of the Hebrew Bible outside Genesis 1 and Ezekiel 1.

terms.rationale: The Hebrew is a construct form without a definite article — בְּרֵאשִׁית means 'in beginning-of', not 'in the beginning' (which would require בָּרֵאשִׁית). The verse is grammatically a temporal clause introducing v2, not a freestanding declaration. The Rashi-favoured 'when Elohim began to create' reading (also adopted by NJPS, NRSV, and the major modern scholarly translations) preserves this structure. The traditional 'In the beginning' is a Christian-tradition reading that fixes a doctrine of creatio ex nihilo onto a Hebrew that doesn't grammatically support it. Restoring the construct sharpens the WoH lens: the text describes a project beginning, not the absolute origin of all matter.

The form is grammatically masculine plural (-im ending). The translation 'God' (singular) is a theological smoothing inherited from the LXX's choice to render with singular Θεός and confirmed by Vulgate's Deus. The plural form is preserved as 'Elohim' (untranslated) per existing WoH convention (see content/i18n/glossary.json #elohim). Plural verbs and pronouns governing Elohim are flagged in their own entries — see na'aseh.

**Article convention.** In narrative passages the bare proper-name form 'Elohim said' reads to English speakers as a singular proper noun (like 'Yahweh said'), which silently re-imports the singular smoothing the WoH translation is explicitly rejecting. Rendering as 'the Elohim said' treats Elohim as a plural class-noun in English (parallel to 'the Egyptians', 'the Anunnaki') and carries the plural sense visibly in every occurrence. The article is dropped only inside the compound proper name YHWH-Elohim (Genesis 2:4 onward), which functions as a single divine name. Pronouns and verb agreement still mirror the Hebrew: where the Hebrew governs Elohim with singular verbs/pronouns (e.g., בְּצַלְמוֹ b'tsalmo 'in his image' in 1:27), the English keeps the singular ('his/he'); where the Hebrew breaks into plural (e.g., the cohortative נַעֲשֶׂה na'aseh 'let us make' in 1:26), the English follows ('us/our'). The agreement seam is the text's own, preserved rather than smoothed.

בָּרָא in the Qal is one of three distinct creation verbs in Genesis 1 (with עָשָׂה asah 'make' and יָצַר yatsar 'form'). All three are flattened to 'create/make' in the canonical English translations, losing a deliberate distinction. בָּרָא specifically denotes shaping or fashioning — its qal usage is reserved for Elohim's action throughout the Hebrew Bible (Gesenius §17), which has often been read theologically as 'create ex nihilo'. The lexical core, however, is the act of shaping — compare the cognate בָּרָא in the Piel meaning 'cut out, fashion'. Rendering as 'shape' preserves the verb's distinctness from asah without inserting a metaphysical claim either way.

עָשָׂה is the general Hebrew verb of making/doing across the corpus. In Gen 1 it is used where bara would be theologically over-specified — for the firmament (v7), the two lights (v16), the beasts (v25), and humanity (v26 'let us make'). The standard 'made' is correct; the choice point is keeping it lexically distinct from bara/yatsar so the reader can see the deliberate verb pattern. Where the canonical translations interchange bara and asah ('created the great sea-monsters' / 'made the firmament') the WoH translation preserves them apart.

שָׁמַיִם is grammatically dual (the -ayim ending). Hebrew has no singular form for sky/heaven — the dual is the lexical default. Rendering as singular 'heaven' (as KJV/ASV do) imports a later theological 'heaven-the-realm' concept onto a word that simply means 'the skies' in the physical sense. 'Skies' is closer to the cosmological register Gen 1 actually operates in and preserves the dual implication. Where the text demands a more numinous register (e.g. כֹּכְבֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם 'stars of the skies'), the rendering scales accordingly.

אֶרֶץ in Hebrew covers a spectrum from 'patch of ground' to 'inhabited territory' to 'the whole inhabited world'. The capital-E 'Earth' (the planet) is a 17th-century-onwards English meaning that has no clean Hebrew equivalent. In Gen 1, אֶרֶץ is paired with שָׁמַיִם — they're a hendiadys for 'the whole physical setting'. 'Land' preserves the Hebrew register; 'earth' (lowercase, when the meaning is the dry ground as opposed to seas) is acceptable in some verses, but the default WoH choice is 'land'. Where the verse explicitly contrasts 'land' (dry) with 'seas' (water) (e.g. v10), 'land' is the more accurate choice anyway.

תֹהוּ וָבֹהוּ is an alliterative pair found together only here and in Jer 4:23 (where it describes a depopulated post-disaster landscape) and Isa 34:11 (similar). The KJV 'without form, and void' renders the pair philosophically (matter without form, Aristotelian); the Hebrew is concrete — a desolate, empty terrain. 'Wasteland and emptiness' preserves the alliterative pair (the -au / -ohu echo) and the concrete imagery. Cf. NJPS 'unformed and void', NRSV 'formless void' — closer to KJV than to the Hebrew. For the WoH lens, the choice matters: a 'wasteland and emptiness' is a pre-project site, not a metaphysical pre-form-and-matter chaos.

תְהוֹם is etymologically and conceptually cognate with Akkadian tiāmtu (cf. Tiamat of Enuma Elish, where the goddess is the primordial salt-sea). The Hebrew has demythologized the term but the word is preserved. 'The deep' is the standard rendering and is correct. The WoH note pointing readers to the Tiamat parallel belongs in the methodology page, not in the translated text.

רוּחַ has three lexical fields: 'breath', 'wind', 'spirit' — the choice is context-driven and Hebrew does not disambiguate. The KJV/ASV 'Spirit of God' is a Trinitarian-tradition reading retrojected onto Gen 1:2; the Hebrew supports 'wind' (cf. NJPS 'a wind from God'), 'breath' (cf. several modern translations), or 'spirit' (the inherited choice). For the WoH lens, 'breath' is preferable: it preserves the physical/atmospheric register the verse operates in and avoids importing a doctrinal claim. The hovering verb in the next clause (merachefet, see entry) supports a physical reading — wind/breath hovers over water; 'Spirit' does not, in any natural register.

רחף in the Piel means to hover or flutter — Deut 32:11 uses it of a mother eagle over her young. The KJV 'moved upon' loses the specifically hovering aerial sense. 'Was hovering over' is the consensus modern reading (NJPS, NRSV, ESV) and preserves the bird-image. Combined with the ruach='breath/wind' reading in the same verse, the image is concrete and physical.
The jussive form expresses a command/wish. 'Let there be' is correct and is the standard rendering. Preserved unchanged.

Standard rendering, unchanged. Note for methodology: the light of v3 is separate from the sun and moon of v14-16 (which haven't been positioned yet at v3). This is a textual feature the WoH lens should surface in the methodology, not paper over.

The formulaic phrase that closes each creation day. 'Good' is the correct rendering of טוֹב here. Preserved unchanged. The methodology note will track the formulaic structure — six days, six closings, with variations on day 2 (no 'good') and day 6 ('very good') as deliberate signals.

Standard rendering. The 'how long is a day' question is a methodology-page discussion, not a translation choice — the Hebrew says יוֹם and the rendering says 'day'. The recurring formulaic close (vayehi erev vayehi voqer yom X 'and there was evening and there was morning, day X') is a structural feature the methodology surfaces.

רָקִיעַ derives from the verb רקע 'to beat/hammer out (metal)'. The LXX renders στερέωμα 'solid/firm thing', whence Vulgate firmamentum, whence English 'firmament'. The Hebrew picture is of a solid dome hammered out across the sky — consistent with the ANE cosmology of a vault above the earth holding back the upper waters (cf. v6-7, where the raqia is explicitly placed 'between waters and waters'). 'Dome' (cf. NRSV) preserves this; 'firmament' is technically defensible via Latin but obscures the imagery; 'expanse' (ESV, NIV) is a modern softening that papers over the solid-dome cosmology. The WoH choice is 'dome' — accurate to the Hebrew picture; whether the picture corresponds to physical reality is a separate question the methodology can address.
Hebrew מַיִם is grammatically dual; there is no singular form. 'Waters' is correct; the plural carries.

מְאֹרֹת is a more technical word than אוֹר — it denotes a light-source object, not light itself. 'Luminaries' captures this; 'lights' (KJV/ASV) is acceptable but loses the source-object register. The methodology notes the deliberate avoidance of שֶׁמֶשׁ shemesh ('sun') and יָרֵחַ yareach ('moon') — both of which were divine names in the ANE polemical environment. The text describes the sun and moon as functional luminaries, not as the named deities.

terms.literal: the great luminary / the small luminary
terms.standardEnglish: the greater light / the lesser light
terms.wohChoice: the greater luminary / the lesser luminary
terms.rationale: The text deliberately refrains from using שֶׁמֶשׁ shemesh ('sun') and יָרֵחַ yareach ('moon') in this verse. Both shemesh and yareach were divinized in surrounding ANE traditions (Shamash, Sin, etc.). Genesis 1 names them only by function — 'the greater luminary' to rule day, 'the lesser luminary' to rule night. This is a deliberate polemical structure and preserving it in translation is non-optional for the WoH lens.

מֶמְשֶׁלֶת is a relatively technical political term — 'governance' or 'dominion' over a territory. 'Rule' (KJV) is acceptable but more general. The luminaries are explicitly granted מֶמְשֶׁלֶת — functional jurisdiction over day and night. 'Governance' surfaces the structural language of the verse: the cosmos is being organized into administrative jurisdictions assigned to functional bodies.

Standard rendering. The luminaries function as signs (ototh) and appointed times (moadim) — a technical, calendrical function. The methodology may note that this structural-instrumental role for the celestial bodies is part of the polemical demythologization of sun/moon/stars.

מוֹעֲדִים specifically denotes fixed times — festivals, appointed assemblies, predetermined occasions. The KJV/ASV 'seasons' is a natural-cycles reading (spring/summer/autumn/winter); the Hebrew is more specifically calendrical — the technical liturgical and astronomical fixed-time vocabulary. 'Appointed times' preserves the precision and the calendrical-instrumental role of the luminaries.

תַּנִּינִם is the standard Hebrew Bible word for the serpent-dragon class of creatures. BDB s.v. תַּנִּין: 'sea-monster, serpent, dragon'; HALOT: 'sea-monster, dragon, snake'. The lexical core is the serpent-dragon register; the word itself contains no aquatic morpheme — the sea-association in Gen 1:21 is positional (the verse sits between the swarming-waters of v20 and the blessing-of-the-seas of v22), not lexical. KJV renders the same word 'dragon(s)' across the corpus (Deut 32:33, Ps 91:13, Isa 13:22, Isa 27:1, Jer 9:11, Ezek 29:3, Mal 1:3, etc.); only Gen 1:21 gets the euphemistic 'whales'. Modern translations soften to 'sea-monsters' (ASV, NJPS, NRSV) or 'great sea-creatures' (NIV, ESV, Alter) for Gen 1:21 specifically, but the lexical case for the dragon rendering is the same here as in the prophets. The text is explicitly performing a Chaoskampf demythologization at this verse: the same dragon-class beings deified across the ANE — Akkadian Tiamat (etymologically cognate with תְהוֹם tehom in Gen 1:2), Ugaritic Lotan / Hebrew Leviathan, Egyptian Apep — are something Elohim shaped, and the deliberate switch from asah to bara (cf. the bara entry) is the demythologizing move. Translating as 'sea-creatures' obscures the polemic the verse exists to perform. Rendering as 'great dragons' preserves the lexical register and makes the demythologization visible in the translation itself: the reader sees the dragon-class creatures named, and sees Elohim shaping them rather than fighting them. Reclassified from direct to inferred in v2.0.0 because the choice between 'dragons' and the modern translation tradition's 'sea-creatures' / 'sea-monsters' is a genuine interpretive call.

terms.literal: living being / living throat / living self
terms.rationale: נֶפֶשׁ is famously hard — its semantic core is 'breath/throat/self/life', not a separable immortal soul. The KJV 'soul' (in Gen 2:7 for adam) is a Platonic-dualist reading retrojected; modern Hebrew Bible studies consensus is firmly 'being' or 'creature'. 'Living being' preserves the Hebrew register without inviting the Platonic reading.

מִין is 'kind' / 'species' in a non-modern-biological sense — a category of living things. 'According to its kind' is the standard modern rendering. The phrase recurs as a refrain through the creation of plants and animals and surfaces the categorical / sortal structure of the creation account.

נַעֲשֶׂה is the cohortative first-person *plural* of עָשָׂה — 'let us make'. This is grammatically unambiguous and is the central textual datum for any reading of Elohim as more-than-one. Standard theological glosses include (i) the royal/plural of majesty (ad hoc and not well-attested elsewhere in Hebrew for divine speech), (ii) Trinitarian pre-figuring (Christian retrojection), (iii) divine-council reading (cf. Job 1, Ps 82 — the consensus modern scholarly view), or (iv) the plural is to be read at face value (the WoH lens). The translation does not adjudicate; it preserves the plural literally — 'Let us make humankind'. The methodology page argues for the WoH reading.

אָדָם adam: human/humankind, not a personal name in Gen 1 (the named individual Adam appears later). Gendered KJV 'man' is corrected to 'humankind' (also NJPS/NRSV).

צֶלֶם elsewhere denotes a physical statue or representation (Num 33:52, 1 Sam 6:5 — 'golden images'). דְּמוּת denotes a likeness or resemblance (Ezek 1:5,10 — 'a likeness as the appearance of a man'). The two words together emphasize physical resemblance and form, not abstract attribute. The traditional Jewish and Christian readings (man bears Elohim's spiritual or moral image) are interpretive extensions; the Hebrew lexicon supports a physical-resemblance reading directly. 'Image / likeness' is preserved as the standard rendering; the WoH methodology surfaces the physical-resemblance lexical core, which is central to the Elohim-as-physical-beings reading.

terms.source: וַיִּבְרָא אֱלֹהִים אֶת־הָאָדָם בְּצַלְמוֹ ... זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה בָּרָא אֹתָם
terms.translit: vayivra elohim et-ha'adam betsalmo ... zachar uneqevah bara otam
terms.literal: and-shaped Elohim humankind in-his-image ... male and-female he-shaped them
terms.standardEnglish: So God created man in his own image ... male and female created he them
terms.wohChoice: And Elohim shaped humankind in his image ... male and female he shaped them
terms.rationale:
Tension point: v26 has plural 'let us make' (na'aseh) but v27 has singular 'in his image' (betsalmo). The shift is real and is one of the textual features the methodology must surface — the WoH reading treats it as the difference between the deliberative project speech (plural) and the actual executing action (singular agency assigning the project) within the Elohim group. The standard translations preserve the singular here without comment; the WoH translation preserves it too but flags it in methodology.

זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה zachar uneqevah: male and female, biological-anatomical terms. The verse asserts a dimorphic human creation at the original act, not a sequential creation (Gen 2 will sequence). Preserved standardly.
Standard rendering. The imperative pair is a deliberate parallel structure — preserved.

כבשׁ in qal: subdue, conquer, bring under. רדה in qal: rule, dominate, tread (in a winepress). Both verbs are strong and have been the subject of intense exegetical debate in environmental ethics (Lynn White Jr. 1967 thesis and after). The Hebrew verbs are not softened. The KJV 'have dominion' for redu is acceptable; 'rule' is closer. The WoH choice preserves the strength of both verbs without softening — the methodology page can address the interpretive question.
The closing formula of day 6. Standard rendering. Note the deliberate intensifier meod — six days of 'good' and a final 'very good' as a structural peak. The methodology surfaces this.

terms.standardEnglish: And the evening and the morning were
terms.wohChoice: And there was evening and there was morning
terms.rationale: Formulaic close of each numbered day. The 'evening first' ordering reflects the Hebrew calendrical convention (day begins at sunset). The repeated 'and there was X' (vayhi) preserves the cadence; the KJV 'And the evening and the morning were' loses the doubled vayhi structure. Modern translations and the WoH translation both preserve the doubled structure.

יָצַר is the third of Genesis' creation verbs (alongside בָּרָא bara 'shape' and עָשָׂה asah 'make'). It is the potter's verb: same root as יוֹצֵר yotser 'potter' (Jer 18:2-6). The image is hands-on, material, shaping clay. Translating as 'form' preserves the manual register and distinguishes it from bara (categorical fashioning) and asah (general making). The verb anchors Genesis 2's anthropological account: YHWH-Elohim 'forms' the human from dust (v7) and 'forms' the animals from the ground (v19) — the same verb for the same kind of act.

The compound name appears for the first time in Gen 2:4, marking the shift from the Priestly creation account (ch. 1, 'Elohim') to the Yahwist anthropological account (ch. 2-3, 'YHWH Elohim'). Standard English renders יְהוָה as 'the LORD' (small caps), following the Masoretic Qere-perpetuum (read 'Adonai' for the unpronounced Tetragrammaton) and the LXX's κύριος. This is a Second Temple-era reading convention, not a translation. The Hebrew is a proper name, the four consonants YHWH, traditionally Yahweh. WoH preserves the unpronounced consonants (YHWH) and the unsmoothed plural (Elohim), reflecting the seam between the two creation accounts rather than smoothing it under a single English 'God' or 'LORD'.

תוֹלְדוֹת from יָלַד 'to give birth' is the genealogical formula structuring the book of Genesis (2:4, 5:1, 6:9, 10:1, 11:10, 11:27, 25:12, 25:19, 36:1, 37:2). The literal sense is 'begettings' — what comes forth from. KJV/ASV 'generations' carries the right structural sense but reads archaic. NJPS 'this is the story of' and Alter 'this is the tale of' capture both the narrative and genealogical function. 'Tale' preserves the seam-marker function while reading clean in English. The first toledot (2:4) is structurally unusual: it births the skies and the land themselves, treating cosmos as a lineage.

terms.partOfSpeech: noun, masculine singular with definite article
terms.standardEnglish: Adam (as a proper name) or the man
terms.rationale: Throughout Genesis 2 and most of Genesis 3, the Hebrew is הָאָדָם — the definite article + adam — which grammatically means 'the human' as a category, not 'Adam' as a personal name. The article disappears (allowing read-as-name) only at Gen 4:25. Inserting 'Adam' as a personal name throughout chapters 2-3 (KJV, ASV, NIV) imports a name where the Hebrew has a category-noun. NJPS and most modern scholarly translations preserve 'the man'. WoH uses 'the human' for two reasons: (a) preserves the categorical-noun grammar, (b) keeps the door open to the wordplay with אֲדָמָה adamah (ground) — the human is the *one from the ground*, not yet sexed and not yet named.

Hebrew אָדָם adam (human) and אֲדָמָה adamah (ground, soil) share a root (אדם, 'red, ruddy') and are deliberately paired in Gen 2:7: the human is dust from the ground. The wordplay is structural — adam is etymologically 'the earth-being'. Standard English ('man'/'ground') buries the play. WoH preserves it as a recurring pair: 'the human' from 'the ground'. Alter's 'human / humus' captures the etymological link but reads awkward; 'human / ground' keeps the register clean while the pairing is documented in commentary.

נְשָׁמָה neshamah is distinct from רוּחַ ruach (breath/wind/spirit, Gen 1:2) and נֶפֶשׁ nefesh (living being, throat, appetite). Neshamah is specifically *exhaled* breath — what is breathed *out*, into nostrils. The verb here is נָפַח napach 'to blow', not נשׁף or נשׁב. The picture is hands-on, mouth-to-nostril, a craftsman animating his work — continuous with the potter's-verb yatsar in the same verse. Standard 'breath of life' is correct; the commentary surfaces the distinct lexeme so the three breath-words (ruach / neshamah / nefesh) don't collapse into one.

The preposition is בְּ be- 'in', not the construct relation that would yield 'of'. Eden is a region; the garden is *in* it. Gen 2:8 reads literally 'a garden in Eden, eastward'. The 'Garden of Eden' (KJV, ASV, NIV) treats Eden as a proper name modifying garden, but the Hebrew has gan 'garden' indefinite + b- 'in' + Eden (place). The original picture is more spatial: there is a place called Eden, and within it YHWH-Elohim plants a garden. Restoring the preposition restores the geography — the cosmic locale is Eden; the gardened part is one feature of it.

terms.source: עֵץ הַחַיִּים / עֵץ הַדַּעַת טוֹב וָרָע
terms.translit: etz hachayim / etz hada'at tov vara
terms.literal: tree of the lives / tree of the knowing of good and bad
terms.standardEnglish: tree of life / tree of the knowledge of good and evil
terms.wohChoice: tree of life / tree of knowing good and bad
terms.rationale: Two notes. First, דַּעַת da'at from יָדַע yada is *experiential* knowing — intimate acquaintance, not abstract cognition (the same verb covers sexual knowing in Gen 4:1). 'Knowing' rather than 'knowledge' keeps the verbal force. Second, טוֹב וָרָע tov vara is the merism for 'everything' — 'good and bad' covers the full moral and practical spectrum (Solomon's request in 1 Kgs 3:9 for the ability to *discern* tov vara is the same idiom and reads as 'wise judgment'). 'Good and evil' (KJV, ASV) imports a Manichaean polarity the Hebrew doesn't have; 'good and bad' is closer to the merism. The tree is the tree of *experiential acquaintance with the full range*.

terms.literal: to work it and to guard it
terms.standardEnglish: to dress it and to keep it
terms.wohChoice: to work it and to guard it
terms.rationale: Two verbs. עָבַד avad covers 'work, serve, till' — the same root as עֶבֶד eved 'servant'. שָׁמַר shamar covers 'keep, guard, watch over' — the same root as שׁוֹמֵר shomer 'guard, watchman'. KJV 'dress and keep' reads agricultural-pastoral; the Hebrew register is closer to 'work the land and guard it'. The human is placed as worker-and-watchman, not as owner. Note also the feminine suffix -ah on both verbs while גַּן gan is masculine — the suffix likely points back to אֲדָמָה adamah (feminine), making this stewardship of the ground itself, not just the gardened parcel.

Distinctively Hebrew construction: the infinitive absolute placed before its cognate finite verb intensifies — the meaning is 'you will most certainly die'. Same construction appears in v16 with אָכֹל תֹּאכֵל akhol tokhel ('you may freely eat'). Standard English 'surely die' is the right register. The interpretive question is *what kind* of death — same-day biological death (which doesn't happen), or a different death (mortality entering, separation from the garden state, the end of the unfallen condition). The grammar settles certainty but not kind; the commentary leaves the question open rather than picking one tradition.

terms.literal: help corresponding to / over against him
terms.standardEnglish: a help meet for him / a helper suitable for him
terms.rationale: Two words, each commonly mistranslated. עֵזֶר ezer is not 'helper-subordinate' — the same noun is used 16 times in the Hebrew Bible for *divine* help (Pss 33:20, 70:5, 121:1-2, etc.). It denotes powerful or critical aid, not assistant-class help. כְּנֶגְדּוֹ kenegdo is from נֶגֶד neged 'opposite, in front of, corresponding to' — the relation is face-to-face equality, not behind-and-supporting. The two words together describe a counterpart of matching standing. 'A help meet for him' (KJV) became 'helpmeet' in English usage and slid further into 'helpmate' — a slide the Hebrew never supports. WoH renders 'a counterpart fitting him' to recover both senses: equal standing (kenegdo) and substantive aid (ezer).

צֵלָע appears 41 times in the Hebrew Bible and *never* means 'rib' outside this passage's traditional reading. It is the architectural term for the *side* of structures: side of the Ark (Ex 25:12, 14), side of the Tabernacle (Ex 26:20, 26, 35), side of the altar (Ex 27:7), side of the Temple's holy place (1 Kgs 6:34). The LXX rendered πλευρά 'side', and the Vulgate costa is itself ambiguous in classical Latin (side or rib). 'Rib' is medieval anatomical guesswork that hardened into tradition. Reading 'side' rebalances the picture: YHWH-Elohim does not extract a single bone but takes one *side* of the human and builds a counterpart — the two becoming halves of a whole, which the wordplay of v23 (etzem 'bone', basar 'flesh') and v24 (basar echad 'one flesh') reinforces.

The wordplay in Gen 2:23 turns on the consonantal near-identity of אִישׁ ish ('man') and אִשָּׁה ishah ('woman') — the etymologies are actually separate (ish from a different root than ishah, whose root is אנשׁ), but the *sound* play is unmistakable, and the verse uses it to name. Most English translations capitalize the names ('Woman ... Man') to signal the naming function. WoH keeps the Hebrew transliterations (Ishah / Ish) in italics in v23 to preserve the sound-play that drives the verse. Elsewhere these words appear in their normal English equivalents.

The phrase concludes the narrative arc that began with the human being divided (tzela 'side' taken from him in v21-22): the marital bond is figured as the *re-unification* of what was divided. בָּשָׂר basar 'flesh' carries the full register — body, kin, common substance — so the phrase covers both physical union and kinship-by-substance. Standard 'one flesh' is correct; the commentary surfaces the structural inversion (one human divided into two becomes two re-merged into one) that the chapter is doing.

תַּרְדֵּמָה is not ordinary sleep (שֵׁנָה shenah). It denotes a divinely-induced trance state, most famously in Gen 15:12, where it falls on Abram during the covenant theophany ('a deep sleep fell upon Abram, and behold, a horror of great darkness fell upon him'). Also in 1 Sam 26:12, Job 4:13, 33:15, Isa 29:10 — always with an oneiric, supernatural, or anaesthetic quality. The verb here is הִפִּיל hippil 'caused to fall', a hiphil — Elohim makes the trance descend. 'Deep sleep' is the conventional rendering and captures the unconsciousness; the commentary surfaces the trance-quality so the reader does not assume natural slumber.

Gen 3:1 opens with a deliberate soundplay: עָרוּם arum 'shrewd, crafty' (describing the serpent) shares the consonants of עֲרוּמִּים arummim 'naked' (the closing word of 2:25, describing the human and his woman). The two adjectives are from different roots etymologically — arum from ערם 'be shrewd', arummim from ערה 'be bare' — but the sound-play is unmistakable and structural: the chapter bridges from a state of nakedness-without-shame to a confrontation with shrewdness-without-disclosure. KJV 'subtil' (now archaic) and later 'crafty/cunning' carry the negative cast that 'shrewd' delivers cleanly without import a moral pre-verdict. The serpent is *cleverer* than any field-beast; whether that cleverness is malicious is the chapter's open question, not its premise.

The serpent's reply to the woman is the exact same intensifying construction as YHWH-Elohim's prohibition in 2:17 (מוֹת תָּמוּת mot tamut 'you shall surely die'), but here with the negation לֹא lo prefixed and the verb in 2mp plural. The placement of לֹא before the infinitive absolute is unusual — typically the negation modifies the finite verb. Reading the syntax strictly: the negation overrides the certainty itself, so 'you shall *surely not* die' is closer than the conventional 'you shall *not surely* die'. The serpent is not saying death is uncertain — he is saying death will certainly *not* happen.

terms.literal: like Elohim / like divine beings / like gods
terms.rationale: כֵּאלֹהִים is the preposition כְּ ke- 'like' attached to אֱלֹהִים Elohim. The phrase is grammatically ambiguous between singular ('like God') and plural ('like divine beings, like gods'), and most modern translations render plural here (NJPS 'like divine beings', NRSV 'like God'). KJV/ASV 'as gods' opted plural; the LXX rendered ὡς θεοί 'as gods', plural. The serpent's claim is that eating opens the eyes and grants knowledge that places the eaters into the Elohim-class. WoH renders 'like the Elohim' — preserving the same noun form used throughout the translation, where 'the Elohim' carries the plural class-noun sense. The serpent's promise is not deification into a single God-state but *upgrading into the Elohim class* — a category-rise.

terms.literal: your eyes will be opened / the eyes of the two of them were opened
terms.standardEnglish: your eyes shall be opened / the eyes of both of them were opened
terms.wohChoice: your eyes will be opened / the eyes of the two of them were opened
terms.rationale: פָּקַח paqach 'open the eyes' is the specific verb for *uncovering perception* — used of opening blind eyes (2 Kgs 6:17, Ps 146:8) and metaphorically of awakening insight. The verb appears twice in the chapter: in the serpent's promise (v5, niphal imperfect 'will be opened') and in the fulfillment (v7, niphal preterite 'were opened'). The promise is precisely kept — eyes do open, perception does shift. What follows that opening is what is contested: not whether they gain knowledge but what knowledge is gained, and what is lost in gaining it.

terms.literal: walking about / strolling in the garden
terms.rationale: The hithpael of הָלַךְ halach gives an iterative or habitual sense: walking back and forth, strolling, going about. The same form describes Enoch's relationship with the Elohim ('Enoch walked-about with the Elohim', 5:22, 24) and Noah's (6:9). YHWH-Elohim's *mithallech* in the garden is a manner of presence — moving through, not arriving or departing. Standard 'walking' is correct in meaning; 'walking about' captures the iterative force and the at-home quality of the divine presence in the garden — the picture is not a visitor descending but the garden's resident moving through its grounds.

terms.literal: to/at the wind of the day
terms.standardEnglish: in the cool of the day
terms.wohChoice: at the breeze of the day
terms.rationale: רוּחַ ruach here is the same word that meant 'breath/wind/spirit' in 1:2. In construct with יוֹם 'day', the phrase denotes the *wind associated with a part of the day* — Mesopotamian and Levantine convention reads it as the evening breeze, when the heat of the sun gives way and air movement returns. KJV 'cool of the day' is a free rendering of the implied climatic reference; 'breeze of the day' is closer to the actual word while letting the climatic sense show through. Some readings push further — *ruach hayom* could be 'at the spirit of the day' (the day's own *ruach*), giving the passage a more numinous register. WoH stays with the breeze reading and lets commentary surface the alternatives.

אַיֶּכָּה is the interrogative אֵי ay 'where?' fused with a 2ms pronominal suffix. The grammar is straightforward, but the *theological* register has been heavily debated — does YHWH-Elohim not know where the human is (rendering the question literal), or is the question rhetorical (already-knowing, summoning the human into self-disclosure)? The Hebrew settles nothing; both readings work. WoH renders the words straight ('where are you?') and leaves the register open. The chapter's chain of questions that follows (v11 'who told you?', v13 'what is this you have done?') reads more naturally as Socratic-investigative than as informational.

terms.literal: he will crush you (regarding the) head / you will crush him (regarding the) heel
terms.standardEnglish: it shall bruise thy head / thou shalt bruise his heel
terms.wohChoice: he will strike you at the head / you will strike him at the heel
terms.rationale: שׁוּף shuf appears only here and in two parallels (Ps 139:11; Job 9:17). The semantic core is contested: 'bruise', 'crush', 'strike', 'lie in wait for'. The same verb governs both clauses, so the action is reciprocal — whatever the seed does to the serpent's head, the serpent does to the seed's heel. The traditional Christian reading (Vulgate ipsa conteret 'she shall crush', with 'she' = Mary) is text-critically unsupported and contributes to a *protoevangelium* tradition the Hebrew does not deliver on its own. WoH renders 'strike' (less anatomically committed than 'crush', less weak than 'bruise') and lets the symmetry of the two clauses stand: head against heel, with mortal stakes for the lower clause (the serpent's head) and risk-of-injury stakes for the upper (the human's heel).

terms.literal: toil, hardship, labor / pain, hurt, sorrow
terms.rationale: Both nouns derive from the root עצב, which spans 'pain, hurt, sorrow, hardship, labor'. The semantic range matters because both nouns appear in close paired use in v16–17: עִצָּבוֹן itzavon to the woman (in childbearing) and to the human (in working the ground), and עֶצֶב etzev to the woman (in childbearing). The pairing is deliberate — both human and woman receive the *same word* for their post-garden condition, distributed differently (her childbearing, his land-working). 'Sorrow' (KJV) is too narrowly emotional; 'pain' is too narrowly physical; 'hardship' captures the spectrum. The shared root binds the two human consequences: the curse falls not on the human or the woman but on their *contexts of generative labor* (childbearing and land-tilling), and the *word for hardship is one*.

תְּשׁוּקָה appears three times in the Hebrew Bible (Gen 3:16, 4:7, Song 7:11). The semantic field is contested. The traditional rendering 'desire' (KJV, ASV) has been read across a spectrum: sexual longing (Song 7:11 leans that way), submissive yearning (post-Augustinian Christian tradition), or hostile drive ('its desire is for you' in 4:7 governs sin's drive toward Cain). The grammatical construction in v16 is parallel to 4:7, where the drive is hostile and the second clause is 'but you must master it'. The Cain parallel suggests the woman's *t'shuqah* in v16 may not be a tender longing but a contested impulse toward, paired with the next clause's יִמְשָׁל־בָּךְ 'he will rule over you'. WoH renders 'longing' as the least over-specified word in the range, and lets commentary surface the parallel.

Critical structural observation: in the three curse-speeches of Gen 3:14–19, the curse is pronounced on the serpent (v14, ארור אתה 'cursed are you'), on the ground (v17, ארורה האדמה 'cursed is the ground'), but *not* on the human and *not* on the woman. The woman receives intensified itzavon in childbearing; the human is told *the ground* he came from is now cursed *on his account* (בַּעֲבוּרֶךָ ba-avurecha). The text refuses to put the human under the curse formula directly. This is structurally precise. The eventual *return to dust* (v19) is not a *new* curse but the human's pre-existing condition surfaced — he was always from the adamah, and now goes back to it.

חַוָּה Chavvah is connected etymologically to חַי chai 'living' / 'life' through assonance, though the actual derivation is debated (some link it to Aramaic חוה 'declare, show'). The text supplies the gloss: 'because she became אֵם כָּל־חָי em kol-chai — mother of all living'. The naming closes a triple of namings: in 2:23 the human names *Ishah*; here in 3:20 he names *Chavvah*; the species itself was named *adam* by the Elohim in 1:26–27. The three namings move from species to gender-pair to individual proper name. WoH keeps the Hebrew form Chavvah (with anglicized Eve flagged in commentary) to preserve the chai resonance. The naming comes *after* the curses — the human's first interpretive act in the post-garden world is to name his woman by *life* rather than by mortality.

כָּתְנוֹת katnot is the plural of כְּתֹנֶת ktonet 'tunic, robe' — the same garment Jacob gives Joseph in 37:3 (the famous ketonet passim, 'tunic of many colors / long-sleeved tunic'). עוֹר or is 'skin, hide' (the homophone אוֹר or 'light' is from a different root, but the sound similarity is sometimes commented on in midrashic tradition — 'garments of light' as a play, though the consonantal forms differ — ע vs. א). The verse marks a major upgrade in human covering: from improvised fig-leaf belts (v7) to YHWH-Elohim-tailored hide garments. The implicit question of where the skins *came from* (the first death of an animal in the narrative) is left silent in the text and noted by tradition.

Gen 3:22 contains the *second* grammatically explicit plural divine speech in Genesis (the first was 1:26 נַעֲשֶׂה na'aseh 'let us make'). YHWH-Elohim says of the human: 'the human has become *like one of us*, knowing good and bad'. The pronoun מִמֶּנּוּ mimmennu is unambiguously 1pl ('from us'), and the cardinal אַחַד echad ('one') quantifies — the human has joined a multi-member category. The verse confirms the serpent's promise (v5: 'you will be like the Elohim, knowing good and bad') was *accurate* — what the serpent foretold has happened, and YHWH-Elohim is now acknowledging it in counsel with the other members of the Elohim-class. This is the chapter's most direct WoH-compatible verse: the divine plural is not a grammatical curiosity but a working multi-being category that the human has now entered the lower tier of.

terms.literal: the cherubim and the flame of the sword the one-that-turns-itself
terms.standardEnglish: the Cherubims and a flaming sword which turned every way
terms.wohChoice: the cherubim and the flaming blade that turns itself
terms.rationale: Two ANE-rooted images. כְּרוּבִים k'ruvim are the composite hybrid guardian-figures of the ancient Near East — winged sphinxes flanking thrones, doorways, and divine spaces (cf. the cherubim of the Ark, Ex 25:18; the cherub-throne in 1 Sam 4:4; Ezekiel's vision in Ezek 10). They are *not* the cherubic putti of medieval and Renaissance Christian art. The plural here suggests a small group, not a pair. לַהַט הַחֶרֶב הַמִּתְהַפֶּכֶת is more striking: לַהַט lahat 'flame, blade' (the word can mean either), חֶרֶב cherev 'sword', and הַמִּתְהַפֶּכֶת ha-mithappechet a hithpael participle 'the one turning itself' / 'the one that revolves'. The picture is a self-rotating flaming blade — possibly an autonomous weapon, possibly a flame that itself takes a sword's form. WoH renders 'the flaming blade that turns itself' to preserve the autonomous-motion image rather than smoothing into 'a flaming sword which turned every way' (KJV, where the sword is held by something else).

terms.partOfSpeech: verb (qal perfect) + noun + direct object marker + proper name
terms.literal: I have acquired/created a man, [with]/[the] YHWH
terms.standardEnglish: I have gotten a man from the LORD
terms.wohChoice: I have made a man — YHWH himself!
terms.rationale: Genesis 4:1 contains one of the strangest grammatical constructions in the book. קָנִיתִי qaniti is qal perfect of קָנָה — semantic range 'acquire, get, possess', but also 'create, beget' (especially of divine creation, e.g., Gen 14:19 'maker of heaven and earth', Ps 139:13). אֶת־יְהוָה et-YHWH is the direct-object marker followed by the Tetragrammaton — grammatically reading 'I have acquired/created a man, YHWH' (in apposition). Most translations finesse this with a preposition: 'with the help of the LORD' (KJV), 'with the LORD' (NJPS), 'with the assistance of the LORD' (Alter). The Hebrew has no preposition; the direct-object marker is plain. The 'help' is supplied to avoid the implication that Chavvah claims to have *given birth to* YHWH. Reading the Hebrew straight, however, opens a remarkable possibility: Chavvah, freshly out of the garden, makes a co-creator claim — the first human birth is framed in language reserved elsewhere for divine creation. Whether the et- is appositional ('a man, YHWH'), associative ('a man with YHWH'), or instrumental ('a man via YHWH') the Hebrew does not adjudicate. WoH renders the verse with the strong reading and lets commentary surface the alternatives.

הֶבֶל is the proper name of Cain's brother and is identical in form to the common noun הֶבֶל hevel — the word that opens Qohelet/Ecclesiastes ('הֲבֵל הֲבָלִים havel havalim, vapor of vapors, all is hevel'). The lexical sense is vapor, breath, fleetingness, futility. Naming the second son *Hevel* is precise foreshadowing — his life will be brief and his death will be unaccounted for in the genealogy. The English form *Abel* derives via the LXX (Ἄβελ) and Vulgate; the Hebrew form preserves the resonance. The chapter is silent on the etymology — unlike its older brother *Qayin* (Cain), whose mother gives an etymological speech in v1, Hevel arrives in v2 with no naming ceremony. The silence itself is striking.

terms.literal: worker of ground / shepherd of flock
terms.standardEnglish: tiller of the ground / keeper of sheep
terms.rationale: The brothers' occupations are described with two construct phrases that frame the rest of the chapter. Cain is עֹבֵד אֲדָמָה oved adamah — the same verb עָבַד avad used of the human in 2:15 ('to work it') and 3:23 ('to work the ground') for the post-garden mandate. Cain is the continuator of the adamah-worker role. Hevel is רֹעֵה צֹאן roeh tzon — shepherd of flock, the pastoral counterpart. The two occupations together represent the foundational economic division of ANE Near Eastern life: settled agriculture vs. nomadic pastoralism. The brothers embody two ways of life, and the chapter's tragedy will choose between them: YHWH regards Hevel's offering from the flock; Cain's offering from the ground is not regarded.

terms.literal: gift, tribute, offering / look at, regard, pay attention to
terms.rationale: מִנְחָה minchah is the generic word for a gift or tribute — used for diplomatic gifts between humans (Gen 32:14), royal tribute (1 Kgs 4:21), and, in cultic context, for a vegetable/grain offering (distinct from the animal זֶבַח zevach). At this point in the narrative, the cultic categories of Leviticus do not exist yet — Cain's offering of fruit and Hevel's offering of firstborn-of-flock are both *minchot* in the generic sense. שָׁעָה shaah is 'to regard, look favorably toward'. The verb is used in v4 with positive sense ('YHWH looked favorably on Hevel and his offering') and in v5 with negation ('but to Cain and his offering he did not look favorably'). The Hebrew gives *no reason* for the differential reception. Tradition has filled the gap heavily (Hevel offered firstlings *and* their fat; Cain merely 'fruit of the ground' without selection — but these inferences add to the text, they do not derive from it). The text leaves the asymmetry as data, not as explanation.

terms.literal: at the door, sin / sin-offering [is] crouching
terms.rationale: Three observations. First, חַטָּאת chatat is grammatically feminine, but the participle רֹבֵץ rovetz is grammatically masculine — a grammatical mismatch suggesting *chatat* is being personified as a male figure (an animal? a demon?) crouching at the door. Second, the verb רָבַץ ravats is used of animals — sheep lying down (Gen 29:2), wild beasts in their dens (Ps 104:22), a wolf in ambush. The picture is bestial-predatory, not abstract. Third, חַטָּאת chatat carries a double range — sin (the act) and sin-offering (the cultic remedy). The double sense gives the verse productive ambiguity: at the door of Cain's life, either *sin is crouching* (about to overtake him) or *the sin-offering is crouching* (available to remedy what's coming). Most modern translations choose 'sin'; the offering reading is older (some rabbinic, some early Christian). WoH renders 'sin couches' (preserving the animal verb) and surfaces the chatat-as-offering reading in commentary on a future Levitical-context verse.

terms.literal: and toward you its longing, and you shall rule over it
terms.standardEnglish: unto thee shall be his desire, and thou shalt rule over him
terms.wohChoice: and its longing shall be for you, and you shall rule over it
terms.rationale: Genesis 4:7's second clause is **verbatim parallel** to Gen 3:16, swapping pronoun referents. In 3:16: אֶל־אִישֵׁךְ תְּשׁוּקָתֵךְ וְהוּא יִמְשָׁל־בָּךְ ('toward your husband [shall be] your longing, and he shall rule over you'). In 4:7: וְאֵלֶיךָ תְּשׁוּקָתוֹ וְאַתָּה תִּמְשָׁל־בּוֹ ('toward you [shall be] its longing, and you shall rule over it'). Same noun *t'shuqah*, same verb *mashal*. The parallel forces a re-reading of 3:16: if *sin's t'shuqah* toward Cain is a hostile/predatory drive that he *must master*, then the woman's *t'shuqah* toward her husband (3:16) is likely also a contested, predator-prey-shaped drive — *not* a tender romantic longing. The two verses comment on each other. WoH renders both with 'longing' (the most lexically neutral option) and lets the verse-pair declare its own meaning by being read together.

terms.literal: bloods of your brother [are] crying out
terms.standardEnglish: the voice of thy brother's blood crieth
terms.wohChoice: the voice of your brother's bloods cries out
terms.rationale: Two precise grammatical features that English typically smooths. First, דְּמֵי demei is **plural** ('bloods'), not singular — Hebrew uses the plural for shed blood (as opposed to circulating blood, which would be singular דָּם dam). The plural is a forensic register: blood that has been let, multiple drops/pools, blood-as-evidence. Second, צֹעֲקִים tzo'aqim is **plural participle** ('crying out', plural), agreeing with the plural bloods. The earth has not absorbed the blood; the blood remains as multiple speaking witnesses. Standard English collapses to 'thy brother's blood crieth' (KJV) — singular subject, singular verb. WoH preserves the plurality on both sides: 'bloods cries out' is grammatically odd in English (subject-verb agreement breaks if we pluralize subject), so the rendering keeps singular *cries* but plural *bloods*, signalling the Hebrew's witness-multiplicity.

terms.standardEnglish: cursed art thou from the earth
terms.wohChoice: cursed are you, from the ground
terms.rationale: Genesis 4:11 is the **first** time the curse formula אָרוּר arur is pronounced directly on a *human*. In 3:14 the serpent received it (a non-human); in 3:17 the *ground* received it ('cursed is the ground because of you'). Now Cain receives it directly: 'cursed are you, *from the adamah* which opened its mouth to take your brother's bloods from your hand'. The preposition מִן min carries both 'from' (separative — Cain is cursed *away from* the ground, exiled) and the 'from' of causation ('cursed by the ground' — the ground is the agent). Both readings work. Either way, the structural arc is dark: the *ground* (the human's source-material from 2:7) is now active against him. The fratricide has turned the ground from passive substrate to forensic witness to alienating force.

terms.partOfSpeech: adjective + noun with 1cs suffix + prepositional + infinitive
terms.literal: great [is] my iniquity / punishment from bearing
terms.standardEnglish: my punishment is greater than I can bear
terms.wohChoice: my iniquity is too great to bear
terms.rationale: עָוֹן avon is one of the most semantically loaded nouns in biblical Hebrew, spanning **three** distinct senses: (1) the *act* of sin/iniquity, (2) the *guilt* of sin, (3) the *consequence/punishment* for sin. The lexicon does not split them; Hebrew uses one word for the act, its weight, and its result. The verb נָשָׂא nasa 'to bear, carry, lift' is similarly multivalent — it covers both 'bearing the weight of guilt' and 'bearing the punishment' (and, theologically loaded, 'lifting/forgiving the iniquity'). Cain's cry can be read three ways: (a) my *guilt* is too great to bear (remorse), (b) my *punishment* is too great to bear (complaint), (c) my *iniquity is too great to be lifted/forgiven* (despair of mercy). The Hebrew supports all three; tradition has read each. WoH renders 'my iniquity is too great to bear' to preserve the act-and-consequence ambiguity in English, with commentary surfacing the (c) reading as well.

terms.literal: wandering and fugitive / restless and roaming
terms.rationale: Two participles, both from roots of restless motion. נָע na from נוּעַ nu'a 'to move, totter, wander aimlessly'. נָד nad from נוּד nud 'to wander, flee, mourn-with-movement'. The pair is alliterative in Hebrew (na va-nad) and the meaning is reinforced rather than divided — Cain will be in continuous, purposeless motion. The pair is followed in v16 by the place-name נוֹד Nod (same root as nad) — 'the land of Wandering'. The wandering becomes a place; the place becomes Cain's address. The name and the condition fuse. KJV 'fugitive and vagabond' carries the right register but reads archaic; 'wanderer and fugitive' keeps the alliterative pairing while updating the diction.

אוֹת ot is the generic word for 'sign' — a marker, a token, a portent. It covers a wide range: the rainbow as ot (9:13), circumcision as ot (17:11), the Sabbath as ot (Ex 31:13), miraculous portents as otot (Ex 4:8–9). KJV's 'mark' (the famous *mark of Cain*) suggests something inscribed *on* Cain's body, but the Hebrew preposition is לְ l- ('for/to'), not עַל al ('on/upon'). The sign is *for* Cain — given to him, possibly carried by him, possibly placed in his vicinity. The text is silent on *what* the sign is and *where* it is placed. The medieval interpretive tradition that turned the sign into a forehead-brand has shaped English usage; the Hebrew refuses to specify. WoH renders 'a sign for Cain' and preserves the openness.

terms.partOfSpeech: noun + place name + construct + place name
terms.literal: land of Wandering, east of Eden
terms.standardEnglish: the land of Nod, on the east of Eden
terms.wohChoice: the land of Nod — Wandering — east of Eden
terms.rationale: נוֹד Nod is morphologically identical to the participle נָד nad 'wandering' from v12. Cain is sentenced to be *na va-nad* (wanderer-fugitive) and then settles in *eretz Nod* — the land *called Wandering*. The place-name turns the condition into geography: Cain's punishment becomes his address. *Qidmat Eden* — east of Eden — relocates him spatially in relation to the garden (cf. 3:24, the cherubim are placed at the *east* of the garden of Eden; Cain settles in the same eastern compass-direction). The garden is now west of Cain. The west-east axis tracks: humans began in Eden, were placed in the garden (within Eden, eastward, 2:8), driven out east-ward where the cherubim guard, and now Cain has continued the eastward motion. The pattern is exilic in direction.

Genesis 4:23–24 is the first **poem** in the Hebrew Bible. It is structured as a chiastic boast: Lamech addresses his two wives (*hear my voice, give ear to my speech*), names the violent act (*a man I have killed for wounding me, a child for bruising me*), and concludes with a multiplication on Cain's vengeance-formula ('if Cain is avenged sevenfold, then Lamech seventy-and-sevenfold'). The escalation is structural: the line of Cain moves from one fratricide to one act of disproportionate retaliation, with the multiplier increasing from 7× (the divine protection-formula given to Cain) to 77× (Lamech's self-arrogated revenge-formula). The poem signals the moral trajectory of the Cain genealogy without commentary — by structure alone.

Like the other names of Genesis 4 (Cain ← qaniti, Nod ← nad), Shet is given with an etymological gloss: כִּי שָׁת־לִי אֱלֹהִים זֶרַע אַחֵר ('for the Elohim has appointed for me another seed'). The verb שָׁת shat from שִׁית shit 'to place, set, appoint' shares the consonants with שֵׁת Shet. The English form *Seth* derives from the LXX (Σήθ); the Hebrew form preserves the etymological link. Notable here: the speech is Chavvah's (the second time she speaks etymological in the chapter — first with Cain in v1, now with Shet), and her invocation has shifted from יְהוָה YHWH (v1, 'I have acquired a man — YHWH') to אֱלֹהִים Elohim (v25, 'the Elohim has appointed'). The vocabulary of divine reference is itself a meaningful trace.

terms.literal: then it began [was begun] to call on the name of YHWH
terms.standardEnglish: then began men to call upon the name of the LORD
terms.wohChoice: then it began to be called on the name of YHWH
terms.rationale: The verb הוּחַל huchal is **hophal** (passive of the hiphil) of חָלַל chalal in the 'begin' sense — passive, impersonal: 'a beginning was made'. There is no explicit subject ('men', 'they', 'people'); the construction is intentionally subjectless, akin to 'it was begun'. KJV supplies the subject ('then began men') to make English natural; the Hebrew is more open — *some* calling-on-the-name began at this point, perhaps a cultic practice, perhaps a public utterance, perhaps something less formal. The verse closes chapter 4 by signaling that the calling-on-the-name of YHWH — the practice that will become normative Israelite worship — has its etiological origin in the Shet line, *not* the Cain line. The two genealogies of the chapter (Cain's, vv. 17–22; Shet's, vv. 25–26) have parted ways: Cain's culminates in metallurgy and the vengeance-song; Shet's culminates in the calling-on-the-name.

terms.literal: book/record of the begettings of Adam
terms.standardEnglish: the book of the generations of Adam
terms.wohChoice: the record of the begettings of Adam
terms.rationale: Genesis 5:1 opens with a formula slightly different from the other ten toledot-formulae in Genesis: only here is the introduction *sefer toledot* — 'book/record of the begettings' rather than the simple 'these are the toledot of …'. The word סֵפֶר sefer denotes a written record, scroll, or document — the earliest possible signal of an embedded source-document inside the text. Some scholarly readers take this as evidence the chapter is a once-independent genealogical source incorporated into Genesis. Whatever the source-critical position, the verse signals a new register: the rest of Genesis 5 will follow a stereotyped pattern (born X, lived Y years, fathered Z, lived after Z W years, fathered other children, died, aged total) — the format of a list-document. אָדָם adam here has decisively become the proper name *Adam* (no article), continuing the transition that began in 4:25.

terms.literal: in the likeness of Elohim he made him
terms.standardEnglish: in the likeness of God made he him
terms.wohChoice: in the likeness of Elohim he made him
terms.rationale: Genesis 5:1–2 deliberately recapitulates 1:26–27 in compressed form, using the second of the *image/likeness* pair — דְּמוּת demut 'likeness' — rather than the first (צֶלֶם tselem 'image'). The condensed recall serves the toledot-header's function: it grounds the genealogy in the original making-act. Notably, the verse pairs *bidmut elohim* with the verb עָשָׂה asah 'made' (rather than בָּרָא bara 'shaped' used in 1:27) — a small lexical choice that aligns chapter 5's anthropology with the broader making-language of the chapter (cf. 5:3, *Adam fathered in his own likeness, like his image*). Standard 'God' here is rendered *Elohim* throughout WoH to maintain the plural-class noun consistently. See `tselem-demut` for the v1:26 treatment.

terms.literal: and he called their name Adam
terms.wohChoice: and he called their name Adam
terms.rationale: A grammatical curiosity that English typically misses: שְׁמָם shemam is 'their name' — third-person masculine plural pronominal suffix on a singular noun. *Their name [singular] [is] Adam*. The pair are given a *single* shared species-name, *adam* — recapitulating 1:27's pattern where the species-category-noun applies to both. The grammar makes a quiet point: when the species is named, the proper-noun reading does not yet apply — *adam* is still functioning as 'human' (category) here, even though the same word will function as a proper name from v3 onward. The verse straddles the transition.

terms.literal: in his likeness, according to his image
terms.standardEnglish: in his own likeness, after his image
terms.wohChoice: in his likeness, after his image
terms.rationale: Genesis 5:3 reverses the order of the *image/likeness* pair from 1:26 (*tselem* first, *demut* second) and applies it to a *human* transmission: *Adam fathered* a son *in his own likeness, after his own image*. The phrase that 1:26 used for divine-to-human likeness is now used for human-to-human inheritance. Two readings of the structural significance: (1) the imago is *transmitted* through reproduction — what Adam received from the Elohim he passes to Seth; (2) the Adam-Seth resemblance is the *first instance* of inherited likeness, with the v3 formula serving as the foundational template for biological inheritance. WoH renders both nouns straight and preserves the suffix-doubling: 'in his likeness, after his image'. The pivotal observation — that the imago is *heritable* — is the chapter's quiet theological claim.

The ages in Genesis 5 range from 365 (Enoch, taken early) to 969 (Methuselah, the longest-lived); the non-Enoch average is roughly 907–963. The numbers are presented flat — no commentary, no apology, no symbolic key supplied by the text. Three principal interpretive frameworks exist. (1) **Literal**: pre-Flood conditions (atmospheric pressure, biological state, ancestral genetics) supported lifespans now lost. (2) **Symbolic/numerological**: the numbers encode theological or astronomical meanings — e.g., Enoch's 365 = solar year. The numerical chains have catchable patterns (Methuselah's 969 = the year of the Flood in the chronological arithmetic of the chapter). (3) **ANE comparative**: the Sumerian King List records pre-flood reigns of tens of thousands of years (28,800; 36,000; 43,200); Genesis' Mesopotamian neighbors had vastly longer numbers. Genesis 5's ages, however striking by modern standards, are *demythologized* relative to those traditions — already drastically scaled down. The 600-year Adamic span and the dropping ages after the Flood (Gen 11) align with a structural pattern of pre/post-Flood divergence that the ANE materials share. WoH renders the numbers as the text gives them and surfaces the three reading frameworks in commentary; the chapter does not need to be defended or explained-away to remain coherent.

terms.partOfSpeech: verb (hithpael preterite) + proper name + direct-object marker + noun with article
terms.literal: and Enoch walked-about with the Elohim
terms.wohChoice: Enoch walked about with the Elohim
terms.rationale: Three notable features. First, the verb is הִתְהַלֵּךְ hithallech (hithpael), the *same form* used in 3:8 of YHWH-Elohim *walking about in the garden*. Genesis is using a deliberate verbal echo: what YHWH-Elohim did *in the garden* (3:8), Enoch does *with the Elohim* (5:22, 24). Enoch is *with* the Elohim in the way the Elohim was *in* the garden — habitually moving, present, at-home. Second, the direct-object marker אֶת et governs הָאֱלֹהִים ha-elohim (with article — *the Elohim*) — Enoch is not walking *with* (a preposition that would be עִם im or אֵת et meaning 'with') but walking *the* Elohim — possibly read as 'walking in the company of', possibly as a stronger 'walking accompanying', possibly even 'walking on behalf of'. Most translations render 'walked with God'; the Hebrew is a touch more intimate, the construction unusual. Third, the *direct-article* on Elohim (הָאֱלֹהִים, the-Elohim) is the same form used in Genesis where Elohim functions as a definite category-noun rather than a quasi-proper-name. WoH renders 'Enoch walked about with the Elohim' and notes the et-construction in commentary.

terms.partOfSpeech: negative particle + 3ms suffix + conjunction + verb + direct-object marker + noun
terms.literal: and he is not, for the Elohim took him
terms.standardEnglish: and he was not; for God took him
terms.wohChoice: and he was not, for the Elohim took him
terms.rationale: Enoch is the only patriarch in Genesis 5 whose final disposition is not 'and he died' (וַיָּמֹת vayamot). The chapter's stereotyped death-formula is **broken** for him alone: instead, *v'einennu ki laqach oto elohim* — 'and he was not, for the Elohim took him'. The verb לָקַח laqach is the same verb used in 2 Kgs 2:9-11 for Elijah's ascension ('YHWH was about to *take* Elijah up to heaven by a whirlwind'). The Enoch and Elijah passages are the two clearest cases in the Hebrew Bible of bodily removal-without-death, and they share vocabulary. Later texts (1 Enoch, Jubilees, the Watchers tradition) build extensive cosmologies on this single broken-pattern verse. The Hebrew itself records only: he was not, for the Elohim took him. The text says nothing about destination, nothing about manner. The silence is at least as significant as the taking.

מְתוּשֶׁלַח is morphologically מְתוּ-שֶׁלַח — mtu (man, mortal, from a rare root) + shelach (dart, weapon, or the verb 'send'). The most literal reading is 'man of the dart/weapon' — a warrior-name. But the verb root שָׁלַח shalach also means 'send forth, dispatch' — opening a second reading: 'when-he-dies-[it]-will-be-sent', referring (in midrashic tradition) to the Flood. The arithmetic is famously suggestive: Methushelach is 187 when he fathers Lamech (5:25); Lamech is 182 when he fathers Noah (5:28); Noah is 600 when the Flood begins (7:6). 187 + 182 + 600 = 969 — exactly Methushelach's recorded lifespan (5:27). The midrashic reading (Rashi) takes this as deliberate: Methushelach's death is the precondition for the Flood. WoH renders the Hebrew form *Methushelach* and surfaces both readings.

terms.partOfSpeech: proper name / verb (piel imperfect 3ms with 1cp suffix)
terms.rationale: Lamech (the Shet-line Lamech, not the Cain-line Lamech of 4:18–24) names his son נֹחַ Noah and supplies the etymology: זֶה יְנַחֲמֵנוּ zeh yenachmenu — 'this one will comfort us'. The actual etymology of the name *Noach* is more likely from the root נוּחַ nuach 'to rest, settle, come to rest' (giving 'rest, settling') rather than נָחַם nacham 'comfort' (which would give the noun *nacham*). The two roots are related sonically but distinct lexically. The verse is performing a *folk etymology* — connecting Noah's name to *comfort* by sound-association, not strict derivation. The naming-speech also closes a structural arc: Lamech names his son in hope of relief *from the ground YHWH cursed* — the curse of 3:17 returns in the chapter's final naming-speech. The Shet-line acknowledges the curse and names a son in hope of its undoing. The flood narrative will continue the theme: Noah's name carries the chapter's hope, and the deluge will be partially answered in 8:21 ('I will not again curse the ground because of the human').

Genesis 6:2 introduces one of the most debated phrases in the Hebrew Bible. בְּנֵי b'nei is 'sons of' (construct plural); הָאֱלֹהִים ha-elohim is 'the Elohim' (definite). The phrase appears elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible at Job 1:6, 2:1, 38:7 — always referring to *divine beings* attending YHWH's council, never to human Sethites or Israelite rulers. The plain-reading lexical evidence consistently points to a divine class.

Four interpretive traditions have grown up around the phrase. **(1) Fallen angels**: the Second Temple reading (1 Enoch, Jubilees, the Watchers tradition, Jude 6, 2 Peter 2:4), still dominant in Eastern Orthodox and many evangelical readings. **(2) Royal/judicial figures**: the Targum Onkelos renders 'sons of the great ones / nobles', reading the Elohim as human authorities (cf. Ps 82). **(3) Sethite line**: Augustine's reading, dominant in Western Christianity from late antiquity through the Reformation — the *b'nei ha-elohim* are the godly line of Seth, the *b'not ha-adam* the wayward Cain-line. **(4) Divine beings / a class of supernatural agents**: modern critical consensus, with varying degrees of literalism.

WoH renders 'sons of the Elohim' — preserving both the plural class-noun *Elohim* and the construct 'sons of'. The reading is consistent with Genesis 1:26 ('let *us* make'), 3:22 ('like *one of us*'), and 5:24 (Chanoch taken by Elohim): the Elohim is a multi-being class, and 6:2 describes some of them — *their sons* — interacting reproductively with human women. The text does not condemn the union explicitly; the condemnation register comes in v3, where YHWH limits the human lifespan, and v5–7, where YHWH grieves human wickedness. Whether the wickedness is *caused by* the union or *coincident with* it, the text leaves open.

terms.literal: daughters of the human / of humankind
terms.rationale: The phrase pairs symmetrically with *b'nei ha-elohim* — sons-of-Elohim and daughters-of-the-human. Both use the construct chain (sons-of-X, daughters-of-Y) and definite articles. The pair frames the union as a class-crossing: divine males pairing with human females. The article on *ha-adam* preserves the species-noun sense (humankind in general, not the proper name Adam) — though by Genesis 6 the proper-name Adam has been operating since 4:25, the chapter is invoking the *species* once more. The verse is not naming individual human women (Eve has long died, in narrative time) but the broader female human population.

terms.source: וַיִּקְחוּ לָהֶם נָשִׁים מִכֹּל אֲשֶׁר בָּחָרוּ
terms.translit: vayyiqchu lahem nashim mikkol asher bacharu
terms.literal: and they took for themselves wives from any whom they chose
terms.standardEnglish: they took them wives of all which they chose
terms.wohChoice: they took wives for themselves — any they chose
terms.rationale: Two precise observations. First, *vayyiqchu nashim* — *they took wives* — is the standard idiom for marriage in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Gen 4:19, 11:29, 12:19). The verb in itself is not violent; it is the conventional verb for *taking a woman in marriage*. The interpretive question is whether *the Elohim's sons* married the human daughters or *abducted* them — the verb covers both ranges, with context determining tone. Second, *mikkol asher bacharu* — *from any whom they chose* — emphasizes selection without restriction. The marriage / taking is a *broad* phenomenon — any of the human daughters could be chosen. The verb בָּחַר bachar 'choose' is the verb later used of YHWH choosing Israel; here it is used of the Elohim's sons choosing brides. The selection-by-the-superior pattern is paralleled, but inverted: where YHWH chooses Israel covenantally, the Elohim's sons here choose human women erotically. The covenantal verb is doing reproductive work.

terms.partOfSpeech: negation + verb (qal imperfect, contested root) + noun + prep + adverb
terms.literal: my spirit shall not [verb-with-contested-root] in the human forever
terms.standardEnglish: my spirit shall not always strive with man
terms.wohChoice: my breath shall not abide in the human forever
terms.rationale:
The verb יָדוֹן yadon is morphologically unique in the Hebrew Bible. Three roots have been proposed for it. **(1)** דּוּן/דִּין din 'judge, contend' — giving 'my spirit shall not always strive/contend with the human' (KJV, NIV). **(2)** Akkadian danānu 'remain' or related to a hypothetical שָׁכַן shakan 'dwell' — giving 'my spirit shall not always remain/abide in the human' (Speiser, JPS). **(3)** Hebrew נָדַן nadan 'sheath, contain' — giving 'my spirit shall not always be sheathed/housed in the human'. The Hebrew is grammatically unique enough that all three readings have philological support.

The semantic stakes are different. Reading (1) makes the verse a *struggle*: YHWH's spirit fights against the wickedness in humans, will not contend forever. Reading (2) is *withdrawal*: YHWH's life-giving spirit will no longer indwell humans — and the consequence is mortality limited to 120 years. Reading (3) is *containment*: the divine spirit will not be permanently housed in human bodies. WoH renders 'my breath shall not abide' (preserving the same *ruach* lexeme as 'breath' from Gen 1:2 and 2:7, where the divine breath gives humans life) — making the verse a limit on the *duration* of divine breath in human bodies. The mortality of v3 then follows naturally: when the divine breath no longer abides indefinitely, biological lifespan becomes bounded.

terms.standardEnglish: for that he also is flesh
terms.wohChoice: since he is flesh after all
terms.rationale:
Another grammatical hapax. The Masoretic vocalization בְּשַׁגַּם reads as a contraction of בְּ + שֶׁ + גַּם (b- + relative pronoun + 'also'), giving 'in that also he is flesh' — a causal-explanatory connective. **But** other readings have been proposed: (a) the consonants ב-ש-ג-ם can be read as the verb שָׁגָה shagah 'to err, go astray' — giving *b'shaggam* 'in their erring/straying' (i.e., 'on account of their straying he is flesh'). (b) Some textual witnesses read בְּשַׁגָּם differently, or emend to בְּ-שֶׁ-גַם (causal). The standard English 'for that he also is flesh' (KJV) reads the gam particle and adds 'for that' as connective.

The phrase's larger meaning is also contested. *Hu basar* 'he is flesh' contrasts with *ruchi* 'my spirit/breath' in the same verse. The construction *spirit/flesh* binary is doing structural work: YHWH's *ruach* will not abide in *basar* (flesh) forever. Whether the reason is the union with the Elohim's sons in v2 (humans now have hybrid flesh) or simply human mortal-flesh in general — the text holds the ambiguity. WoH renders 'since he is flesh after all' — capturing the causal-but-resigned tone that the gam ('also, after all') seems to add to *hu basar*.

terms.literal: and his days shall be a hundred and twenty years
terms.standardEnglish: yet his days shall be an hundred and twenty years
terms.wohChoice: his days shall be one hundred and twenty years
terms.rationale:
The 120-year number admits two principal readings. **(1) Lifespan limit**: the human lifespan is being capped at 120. Subsequent post-Flood patriarchs do drop progressively (the Gen 11 patriarchs live 148–600, dropping toward 120 by Moses, who dies at 120 — Deut 34:7 — as the *first* person to exactly fit the cap). **(2) Flood countdown**: 120 years is the time remaining until the Flood. The patriarchs in Gen 11 still live well over 120 because the cap was on time-until-Flood, not on individual lifespan.

Both readings have ancient pedigree. Targum Onkelos and Rashi favor (2) — the countdown — citing the parallel of 7:4 ("seven more days and I will cause it to rain") as a similar fixed grace-period. Reading (1) is older still and dominant in many modern English translations. The chapter does not resolve. Whichever reading one adopts, the verse marks a *temporal limit* — divine patience or biological capacity — and frames everything that follows in the chapter as a countdown or a re-calibration.

terms.literal: the fallen ones / the fellers / the giants
terms.rationale:
The morphology of נְפִילִים Nephilim is contested. Two main derivations: **(1)** from נָפַל nafal 'to fall' — Nephilim as 'the fallen ones' (passive sense, those who fell from a higher place) or 'fellers' (active sense, those who cause others to fall, i.e., overthrowers). **(2)** from Aramaic נְפִילָא nefila 'a giant' (the Aramaic loanword sense, possibly via Babylonian *naplu*) — Nephilim as 'giants', the LXX's choice (γίγαντες).

The Nephilim appear only twice in the Hebrew Bible: here (Gen 6:4) and Numbers 13:33 ('we saw the Nephilim there, the sons of Anak from the Nephilim — and we were as grasshoppers in our own sight'). The Numbers passage occurs *after* the Flood, presenting a textual puzzle: how could the Nephilim still be present if the Flood killed everyone except Noah's family? Three resolutions: (i) the Num 13 mention is hyperbolic / proverbial / panicked-spying; (ii) the Flood was localized, not global; (iii) the Nephilim re-emerged through some unspecified mechanism. The chapter's text refuses to comment. WoH renders 'the Nephilim' transliterated and surfaces the Numbers parallel in commentary.

terms.literal: the mighty ones who [were] from of old, men of the name
terms.standardEnglish: mighty men which were of old, men of renown
terms.wohChoice: the mighty ones who were from of old, men of name
terms.rationale: Three phrases in apposition with *the Nephilim*. **(1)** הַגִּבֹּרִים ha-gibborim — 'the mighty ones', the standard term for heroes, warriors, men of power (cf. Nimrod, 'the first *gibbor* on the land', Gen 10:8). **(2)** אֲשֶׁר מֵעוֹלָם asher me-olam — 'who were from of old', from a long-past time — the legendary register. **(3)** אַנְשֵׁי הַשֵּׁם anshei ha-shem — 'men of the name' / 'men of renown'. The phrase שֵׁם shem ('name') here carries the sense of *reputation, lasting reputation* — the same noun whose making the builders of Babel will seek in 11:4 ('let us make a *shem* for ourselves'). The phrase identifies these figures as the *named heroes* of legendary antiquity. The chapter is placing the Nephilim in the same register as the demigods, named heroes, and warrior-founders of ANE mythology — the Mesopotamian *apkallu*, the Greek heroes, the Egyptian pharaonic ancestors. Genesis 6 is connecting its narrative to that broader heroic-age framework, while routing it through its own theological grammar.

terms.literal: YHWH regretted ... and he was grieved to his heart
terms.standardEnglish: it repented the LORD ... and it grieved him at his heart
terms.wohChoice: YHWH regretted ... and he was grieved to his heart
terms.rationale:
The verb נִחַם nicham (niphal of נָחַם) covers 'be sorry, regret, change one's mind, be comforted/console oneself'. KJV's 'repented' carries the right archaic register but reads moralistic in modern English. The plain sense here is *regret*: YHWH is sorry he made humans. The same verb is used in 1 Sam 15:11 ('I regret that I made Saul king') and 1 Sam 15:35 ('YHWH regretted that he had made Saul king'). The Hebrew is unambiguous: YHWH-as-actor *changes-mind / regrets*.

The paired verb עָצַב atsav (hithpael) is 'to be grieved, pained' — same root as *etzev* (3:16, 3:17, 5:29) — the *hardship* word that has run through the human story since the curse. Now the same root is applied to YHWH's own response: he is *grieved*, the same word that named the woman's childbearing pain and the human's working-the-ground pain. The chapter constructs a parallel suffering: the creator and the creature both share the etzev-quality.

The theological-philosophical traditions have spent considerable effort explaining how an omniscient, impassible God can *regret* and *grieve*. The Hebrew makes no such concession. The text uses the vocabulary it uses, and tradition resolves the discord at higher levels of abstraction.

terms.literal: I will wipe out, blot out, erase
terms.rationale: The verb מָחָה machah is the verb for *erasing, blotting out, wiping clean*. It is used of erasing names from a record (Ex 32:32; Deut 9:14; Ps 69:28), of wiping tears from faces (Isa 25:8), of dishes being wiped clean (2 Kgs 21:13). The image is not destruction-with-fire but *erasure-with-cloth* — a clean removal, as one wipes a slate or a record. YHWH's intention in 6:7 is not punishment-as-spectacle but erasure-as-restart. The Flood is registered, in the chapter's vocabulary, as a clean-slate operation. KJV 'destroy' loses the wiping image. WoH renders 'wipe out' to preserve the verb's primary register.

terms.partOfSpeech: direct-object marker + noun + verb (hithpael perfect) + proper name
terms.wohChoice: Noah walked about with the Elohim
terms.rationale: The verbal echo is exact. In 5:22, 24 the chapter says of Chanoch: *vayithallech Chanoch et ha-elohim* — 'Chanoch walked-about with the Elohim'. In 6:9 the chapter says of Noah: *et ha-elohim hithallech Noach* — same verb (hithallech, hithpael of halach), same direct-object construction (et ha-elohim, 'with the Elohim' rather than the more common preposition עִם im), same form, only the word-order reversed. Two pre-Flood figures are described with this exact construction. The pattern is conspicuous: Chanoch (taken before the Flood) and Noah (preserved through the Flood) are the only humans the Hebrew Bible's pre-Flood narrative singles out for this distinct verbal-relational marker with the Elohim. The construction marks them as exceptional in *the same way*, even as their fates diverge.

terms.literal: and the land was corrupted ... and the land was filled with violence
terms.standardEnglish: the earth also was corrupt ... and the earth was filled with violence
terms.wohChoice: the land was corrupted ... and the land was filled with violence
terms.rationale:
Two intertwined diagnoses of the pre-Flood condition.

*Shachat* — שָׁחַת — covers 'destroy, ruin, corrupt, mar' in different stems. The chapter exploits the multivalence brutally. The land is *corrupted* (niphal, passive — v11), *all flesh corrupted its way* (hiphil, causative-active — v12), and YHWH *is about to destroy them* (hiphil participle *mashchitam* — v13). The same root carries the corruption *and* the destruction-as-response. The Flood is the land's own corruption brought to consummation; YHWH's destruction is the same verb the land already enacted on itself.

*Chamas* — חָמָס — denotes criminal violence: oppression, robbery, wrongful injury. Not generic 'wrongdoing' but specifically violence-as-injustice. Ezekiel 7:23 ('the land is full of judgments of bloodshed, and the city is full of chamas') uses the term similarly. The chapter's specific diagnosis is not unrighteousness in some abstract sense but *violence against neighbor*. The Flood is the consequence of violence-saturated land.

קֵץ qets — *end, termination, limit*. The phrase *qets kol basar* is the apocalyptic register inserted at v13: an absolute terminus has come for all flesh. The phrase will be picked up by later apocalyptic traditions (Daniel 12, the New Testament eschatology, the Qumran texts on the 'end of days'). Here at its origin, the phrase names a *first* end — not the eschatological End but the proto-eschatological *end of the world-that-was-before*. The Flood is presented in the chapter's grammar as a real terminus, with continuation through the ark as the only thread.

כָּל־בָּשָׂר *kol basar* — *all flesh* — encompasses humans and animals together. The flesh category here is broader than the human category; the consequence falls on the entire animate biosphere.
